unitTitle: KS4 Using IT in project management (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: What is project management?
lessons.objectives:
I can say what project management is
I can name four project management methods
I can match each method to what it is like
I can choose a sensible method for a project
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: project management · waterfall · agile · hybrid · critical path · project life cycle. TA: everyone has the starter and activity worksheets. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what would you do? Pupils CHOOSE the best first step for a team advert (plan who does what). Support: choose what a project manager does. Core: define project management. Challenge: multi-select what good project management helps with.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — what is project management? Planning, sharing tasks, checking it gets done on time. Three methods came from software; one (critical path) did not.
3. (20–45) You-do — the four methods. Pupils MATCH each method to its description, then SORT facts into waterfall/agile, ORDER the waterfall stages, and fill the blanks. Likely error: thinking waterfall lets you jump back a step → fix-words “one step at a time, in order.”
4. (45–50) Challenge — which method did NOT come from software? (critical path). Why choose hybrid?
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil names one method and what it is like.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Initiate a project
lessons.objectives:
I can analyse a project brief
I can identify the user requirements of a project
I can identify the constraints and risks of a project
I can describe what a feasibility report is for
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: initiate · user requirements · constraints · mitigate · feasibility report. TA: the Delicious Desserts brief ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — the project life cycle. Pupils ORDER the four stages (initiate → plan → execute → evaluate). Support: pick the first stage. Core: define “initiate”. Challenge: multi-select what happens at initiation.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — analyse the brief. Read the Delicious Desserts brief; highlight or list what is asked for (an integrated system, a dessert selector, two posters).
3. (20–35) You-do — requirements vs constraints. Pupils MATCH the key words, then SORT items into user requirement / constraint, and fill the blanks. Likely error: mixing up requirement and constraint → fix-words “requirement = want; constraint = risk.”
4. (35–50) Feasibility report — pupils start the report (intro + user requirements) and screenshot it onto the activity worksheet. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil names one requirement and one constraint.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Delicious Desserts logo (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Initiate and plan — SMART goals
lessons.objectives:
I can turn an aim into smaller objectives
I can write a SMART goal
I can say what each letter of SMART means
I can explain iteration and interaction
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: objective · SMART goal · iteration · interaction. TA: the SMART goals worksheet ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — fill in the blanks. Pupils complete the initiation paragraph (initiation, analyse, requirements, constraints, feasibility, mitigate). Support: pick what a SMART goal is. Core: what does M stand for. Challenge: a repeated stage = iteration.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — from aims to objectives. An aim is the big thing; objectives are the smaller tasks. Worked example on the integrated system.
3. (20–40) You-do — SMART goals. Pupils MATCH each SMART letter to its meaning, SORT goals into SMART / not SMART, write one SMART goal, and fill the iteration/interaction blanks. Likely error: a vague goal like “do the spreadsheet” → fix-words “say WHAT, and by WHEN.”
4. (40–50) Iteration and interaction — interaction = stages flow into each other; iteration = repeating a stage to improve it. Fix-words: “iteration = repeat; interaction = flow between.” Screenshot the SMART goals table.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil reads one SMART goal.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Tools for planning — Gantt and PERT charts
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a Gantt chart shows
I can say what a PERT chart shows
I can label the parts of a PERT chart
I can choose a sensible planning tool
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: dependency · concurrent · contingency · milestone. TA: computers ready; a Gantt and a PERT template. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — match the planning word (dependency, concurrent, contingency, milestone). Support: what a Gantt chart shows. Core: what a PERT chart uses. Challenge: which tool is easier for a short project (Gantt).
2. (10–20) I-do — Gantt chart. Each task is a bar across time; longer bar = more time; milestones and contingency. Show the bar graphic and a real Gantt chart photo.
3. (20–35) PERT chart — circles (nodes) for tasks, arrows for the order with time on them. Pupils LABEL the PERT chart on the worksheet, then ORDER the steps to build one. Likely error: arrows pointing the wrong way → fix-words “arrows point the way the work flows.”
4. (35–50) You-do — SORT Gantt-vs-PERT strengths, then make the plan and justify the choice. Screenshot the plan. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil points to a node and an arrow.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Carrying out a project, part 1 — the integrated system
lessons.objectives:
I can say what an integrated system (workbook) is
I can say what a formula and a function do
I can build part of a spreadsheet workbook
I can format it in the house style
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: formula · function · workbook · sheet. TA: the workbook template shared with everyone. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — match the formula (=SUM, =MIN, =A4*B4, =IF). Support: what a workbook is. Core: what =SUM does. Challenge: which one is a function.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — the integrated system. One workbook = many sheets in one file. Formula = your own calculation; function = a built-in tool. Likely error: forgetting the = → fix-words “every formula starts with =.”
3. (20–45) You-do — build it. Pupils SORT formula vs function, fill the workbook/sheet blanks, then open the template, format it in the house style, and add formulas one sheet at a time. Keep the pace up. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (45–50) Check it — quick check after each sheet; screenshot the workbook.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil shows one working formula.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Carrying out a project, part 1 — starter worksheet.md
Carrying out a project, part 1 — activity worksheet.md

lessons.title: Carrying out a project, part 2 — visual media
lessons.objectives:
I can follow a design plan
I can say what a hierarchy diagram is
I can build the interactive dessert selector
I can make visual media that fits the audience
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: hierarchy diagram · transition · house style · audience · purpose. TA: the build guide and image source shared. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — match the key term (hierarchy diagram, transition, house style, audience). Support: how the selector is built. Core: what a house style is. Challenge: what to plan first for a poster.
2. (10–20) I-do — the dessert selector. A slide deck of either/or questions; buttons hyperlink to the next slide. A hierarchy diagram shows the links — plan before you build.
3. (20–45) You-do — build it. Pupils ORDER the build steps, then build the selector (master slide for house style, buttons, hyperlinks). Likely error: linking a button to the wrong slide → fix-words “read the slide number before you link.” Then test every button. Screenshot the selector.
4. (45–50) The posters — SORT what to plan (audience, purpose, house style) vs what not to; keep house style the same.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil shows one working button.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Carrying out a project, part 2 — starter worksheet.md
Carrying out a project, part 2 — activity worksheet.md
Carrying out a project, part 2 — slides.md

lessons.title: Evaluate a project
lessons.objectives:
I can recall the four stages of the project life cycle
I can test a product using a testing table
I can give and use peer feedback
I can evaluate the success of a project
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: evaluation · testing table · feedback · recommendation. TA: the integrated workbook and the testing table worksheet open. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — ORDER the project life cycle (initiate → plan → execute → evaluate). Support: which stage checks the finished project. Core: what a testing table is for. Challenge: why feedback should be specific.
2. (10–20) Has the project succeeded? Look back at the feasibility report and SMART goals; mark each product done / not done.
3. (20–40) You-do — testing tables. Pupils SORT what goes in a testing table, MATCH the evaluation words, and fill the expected/actual blanks, then write ten tests. Likely error: writing only what happened → fix-words “expected first, then actual.” Screenshot the testing table.
4. (40–50) Peer feedback — say what is good and why, what could be better and how. Use it to improve.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil names one improvement.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Start your own project
lessons.objectives:
I can find the user requirements of a new project
I can spot the constraints of a project
I can sort activities into the right stage
I can start the planning documents
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: initiate · plan · feasibility report · Gantt chart · PERT chart. TA: the Team Outdoor Obstacles brief and templates ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — SORT activities into the four stages (initiate / plan / execute / evaluate). Support: which stage is first. Core: name a planning-stage task. Challenge: multi-select what happens at initiation.
2. (10–20) I-do — the Team Outdoor Obstacles project. They want a sales sheet, a poster and a business card. Read the brief.
3. (20–40) You-do — initiate it. Pupils SORT user requirement vs constraint, fill the analyse/requirements/feasibility blanks, and start the feasibility report. Likely error: jumping to making things → fix-words “initiate first — what do they want?” 25 minutes calm work. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (40–50) Plan it — write a SMART goal; choose a Gantt or PERT chart. Screenshot the feasibility report.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil names one user requirement.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Start your own project — starter worksheet.md
Start your own project — activity worksheet.md

lessons.title: Execute your own project
lessons.objectives:
I can finish my planning documents
I can create the project products
I can develop a testing table
I can use feedback to improve my work
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: execute · sketch · testing table · feedback. TA: the sales sheet template, image source and tracking sheet ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — rate confidence with formulas (scale 1–5); recap the homework formulas. Support: what must happen before evaluation (test). Core: why sketch first. Challenge: why test before the final evaluation.
2. (10–20) Sketch first — the poster and business card; think house style, audience, purpose.
3. (20–45) You-do — execution. Pupils ORDER the execution steps, SORT products vs planning documents, fill the testing-table blanks, then build the sales sheet, poster and business card. Likely error: rushing and skipping a formula → fix-words “build one part, then check it.” Paced in 5-minute countdowns.
4. (45–50) Test and feedback — write at least five tests; swap for peer feedback; screenshot products and testing table.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil shows one product and one test.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Execute your own project — starter worksheet.md
Execute your own project — activity worksheet.md

lessons.title: Evaluate your project and end-of-unit assessment
lessons.objectives:
I can evaluate a completed project
I can explain iteration and interaction
I can answer quiz questions on project management
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: iteration · interaction · evaluation · recommendations. TA: all the project documents and products open; the end-of-unit quiz worksheet ready. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — MATCH iteration / interaction to their meanings, and rate confidence across the unit (scale 1–5). Support: which stage writes the final evaluation. Core: name two parts of a final evaluation. Challenge: explain iteration vs interaction with an example.
2. (5–40) Final evaluation — scope → each stage → recommendations. Pupils need every document and product open. Calm, independent writing. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
3. (40–52) Assessment — the end-of-unit quiz worksheet (folded summative). 20 questions: mostly single-choice, with “tick all” multi-selects for the non-methods, visual-product planning, testing-table headings, and evaluation questions. Calm assessment conditions; read the multi-selects slowly.
4. (52–55) End of unit — recap the journey: methods → life cycle → feasibility report, SMART goals, Gantt/PERT, testing tables. Self-tick the three “I can…”.
Differentiation: the starter is on the SAME task at three depths; the quiz is a shared assessment everyone sits. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
